name: Baby monitors
summary: Audio, video, and wearable baby monitors — safety-adjacent with subscription-entanglement risk.

evidence.claim: AAP (American Academy of Pediatrics) has stated there is no scientific evidence that infant-monitoring consumer wearables reduce SIDS risk; these are wellness devices, not medical devices.
Wi-Fi baby monitors have been repeatedly breached — unauthorized access via default credentials or unencrypted feeds has been widely documented.

criteria.name: wifi_required_for_viewing
criteria.notes: Dedicated (non-Wi-Fi) monitors avoid the hacking risk (E2).

criteria.name: subscription_required_for_features
criteria.notes: Nanit and some others paywall analytics/events behind subscription.

confabulationPatterns.reality: No FDA-approved consumer wearable reduces SIDS risk, per AAP (E1). 'SIDS detection' or 'reduces SIDS risk' is misleading and can create false confidence.
confabulationPatterns.checkPrompt: Is the SIDS-reduction claim supported by FDA clearance and peer-reviewed evidence?

confabulationPatterns.reality: Consumer monitors are not FDA-cleared as medical devices. 'Hospital-grade' is marketing; hospital-grade medical monitors cost 10x and require prescription.
confabulationPatterns.checkPrompt: Is the device FDA-cleared as a medical device or sold as consumer wellness?

confabulationPatterns.reality: 'Encrypted' varies — transport encryption (TLS) is baseline; end-to-end encryption is rarer. Historical breaches are well-documented (E2).
confabulationPatterns.checkPrompt: Is the encryption end-to-end or just transport-layer?

compatibilityQuestions: Wi-Fi or dedicated? Dedicated is more secure against hacking (E2).
Single room or whole-house coverage? Range requirements.
Subscription acceptable? Some monitors paywall key features.

typicalHiddenCosts.name: Subscription for analytics / event history (Nanit, Owlet)
